html:
Jesus is "like Adam" — both from dust Jesus / Christology Moderate Quran 3:59
"Indeed, the example of Jesus to Allah is like that of Adam. He created him from dust; then He said to him, 'Be,' and he was."

What the verse says

The Quran argues Jesus is not divine because Adam — created with no parents at all — also had a miraculous origin, and no one calls Adam God.

Why this is a problem

This argument misses the actual Christian claim. Christians do not base Jesus's divinity on the mechanics of his birth. They ground it in his pre-existence (John 1:1), his authority to forgive sins (Mark 2:5–7), his receipt of worship (Matthew 14:33), and his resurrection (1 Corinthians 15). The virgin birth is a confirming sign, not the basis for divinity. Answering the Christian doctrine by pointing out that Adam was also supernaturally created is like rebutting an argument about the Mona Lisa's uniqueness by noting that other paintings exist. A God genuinely correcting a theology should address its actual claims, not a straw-man version.

Why it fails

The "popular devotion" framing concedes the Quran is responding to a straw-man rather than to the actual Christological argument. Christian theology locates Jesus's divinity in his pre-existent relationship to the Father and his resurrection, not primarily in his birth mechanics. A divine author correcting the Christian tradition should engage the theology Christians actually confess.

Abraham was a "Muslim" — before Islam existed Strange / Obscure Logical Inconsistency Moderate Quran 3:67
"Abraham was neither a Jew nor a Christian, but he was one inclining toward truth, a Muslim [submitting to Allah]. And he was not of the polytheists."

What the verse says

Abraham (c. 2000 BCE) is retroactively classified as a Muslim. Jacob and his sons are similarly described. The claim is that Islam is the restoration of the original Abrahamic religion.

Why this is a problem

Abraham did not practice the Five Pillars. He did not pray five times daily toward Mecca, fast during Ramadan, or recite the shahada. When apologists defend this by saying "Muslim" just means "one who submits to God," they strip the term of any specific content — making the claim linguistically trivial rather than historically informative. The retroactive rebranding of all pre-Islamic righteous figures as proto-Muslims is also deeply offensive to Jewish and Christian traditions that actually descend from Abraham, and is unfalsifiable: any pre-Muhammadan figure can be labeled Muslim without evidence to the contrary.

Why it fails

Abraham in the Hebrew Bible is presented as covenant-maker through specific ritual and genealogical structures (circumcision, land promise, Isaac-lineage) that are continuous with Judaism, not abstracted from it. Claiming Abraham for Islam while defining "Muslim" broadly enough to include him makes the claim unfalsifiable and strips Abraham of his actual historical-religious context.

Muslims are "the best nation produced for mankind" Prophetic Character Treatment of Disbelievers Moderate Quran 3:110
"You are the best nation produced for mankind. You enjoin what is right and forbid what is wrong and believe in Allah. If only the People of the Scripture had believed, it would have been better for them."

What the verse says

Muslims are ranked as the best community God has ever produced. Jews and Christians are told they are inferior communities because they did not accept Islam.

Why this is a problem

This is religious supremacism written into scripture. Unlike New Testament language that describes the Christian community in terms of grace received rather than moral superiority, the Quran positions Muslims as objectively the best group by divine designation. The downstream effects in Islamic law have been concrete: under classical Islamic governance, non-Muslims paid a special tax, were barred from certain roles, and faced legal disadvantages — justified by the principle of Muslim superiority encoded in verses like this one.

Why it fails

The conditional reading has not been the operative application: classical tafsir and popular Muslim discourse applied "best nation" categorically. A scripture that names one religious community as "best of peoples" embeds supremacist framing regardless of the conditional apologetic, and that framing drove fourteen centuries of discriminatory governance.

Battle of Badr — "twice their number" contradicted in same passage Contradiction Moderate Quran 3:13 vs 8:44
"They saw them [to be] twice their [own] number by [their] eyesight." (3:13)
"And [remember] when He showed them to you, when you met, as few in your eyes, and He made you [appear] as few in their eyes..." (8:44)

What the verses say

Two accounts of the Battle of Badr give opposite perceptions: in 3:13, the Muslims saw the enemy as double their own number; in 8:44, both sides saw each other as few. These cannot simultaneously be true of the same observers at the same moment. The Saheeh footnote on 3:13 also concedes the actual Meccan force was three times the Muslim force, not twice — so the verse gets the number wrong as well.

Why this is a problem

An omniscient narrator describing an event He orchestrated should not produce two mutually exclusive perceptions of the same engagement. A human redactor working from conflicting oral traditions about the same battle would produce exactly this inconsistency.

Why it fails

The sequence is textually unsupported — the Quran does not signal a temporal shift, and importing one is special pleading that could rescue any contradiction in any scripture. Even granting the sequence, 3:13's "twice" claim remains factually wrong: the Saheeh footnote itself acknowledges the Meccan force was more than double.

Fabricated quotes attributed to Jews Strange / Obscure Treatment of Disbelievers Moderate Quran 3:181
"Allah has certainly heard the statement of those [Jews] who said, 'Indeed, Allah is poor, while we are rich.'"

What the verse says

Allah has heard Jews say He is poor and they are rich. Similar attributed statements appear at 5:64 ("Allah's hand is chained") and 9:30 ("Ezra is the son of Allah").

Why this is a problem

None of these statements appear anywhere in Jewish literature — not in the Talmud, Mishnah, rabbinic commentary, or any Jewish source before or after the Quran. Jews did not and do not say Allah is poor, that His hand is chained, or that Ezra is God's son. The Quran is placing words in the mouths of Jews that they never said, then condemning them for it. This is theological straw-manning: fabricate a claim, attack it, declare victory. A divine being should not need to misquote His opponents; only a human author working from limited or garbled information would make such errors.

Why it fails

No fringe Jewish source, sect, or text has ever been identified that held any of these positions. The claims are entirely unattested outside Islam. If the quotes were genuine historical statements, some trace would exist in fourteen centuries of extensive Jewish literature.

The Quran has verses "no one knows the interpretation of" Logical Inconsistency Moderate Quran 3:7
"It is He who has sent down to you the Book; in it are verses precise — they are the foundation of the Book — and others unspecific... And no one knows its true interpretation except Allah."

What the verse says

The Quran divides itself into two categories: clear verses (muhkam) and ambiguous ones (mutashabih). The ambiguous ones, it says, are understood only by Allah.

Why this is a problem

The Quran elsewhere claims to be "clear" (5:15), "easy" (54:17), and "an explanation for everything" (16:89). These two categories of claims cannot both be comprehensively true. Practically, every Islamic sectarian dispute — Sunni vs Shia, literalist vs Sufi — invokes ambiguous verses to support opposing positions. A book that admits some of its own statements are uninterpretable cannot also claim to be clear guidance for all humanity.

Why it fails

The Quran's "clear" and "easy" claims are comprehensive, not qualified. Treating the clarity verses as rhetorical overstatement — while treating the ambiguity of 3:7 as precise — is inconsistent reading. The tradition's own internal divisions over 1,400 years of jurisprudence are the evidence that the ambiguity is real and practically unresolvable.

Polygamy permitted — marry up to four wives Women Moderate Quran 4:3
"Then marry those that please you of [other] women, two or three or four. But if you fear that you will not be just, then [marry only] one or those your right hands possess [i.e., slaves]."

What the verse says

A Muslim man may marry up to four wives simultaneously. If maintaining four is too difficult, he may instead have sexual relations with female slaves — stated as a lawful alternative in the same verse.

Why this is a problem

Two separate problems are bundled together. First, polygamy is a permanent male permission with no parallel for women — a structural asymmetry encoded into eternal divine law. Second, the slave-sex clause is not a historical embarrassment the Quran tiptoes around; it is an explicitly authorized alternative, in the same sentence, to plural marriage. Every modern apologist who claims Islam abolished slavery must explain why the divine text explicitly authorized sexual use of slaves as a substitute for plural wives.

Why it fails

The "transitional to monogamy" reading is a 20th-century apologetic innovation without classical support: fourteen centuries of Islamic jurisprudence treated polygamy as a permanent permission. And the slave-sex clause remains: if the Quran intended to push toward monogamy, it should not have retained concubinage as an alternative in the very verse that introduces the four-wife limit.

Adulterous women confined to houses until death — then abrogated Women Abrogation Moderate Quran 4:15 (abrogated by 24:2)
"Those who commit immorality of your women — bring against them four [witnesses] from among you. And if they testify, confine them to houses until death takes them or Allah ordains for them [another] way."

What the verse says

Women proven guilty of sexual immorality by four witnesses are to be imprisoned at home for life. The Saheeh footnote admits this was abrogated by 24:2, which prescribes 100 lashes instead.

Why this is a problem

The verse illustrates the abrogation problem while adding a gender asymmetry: the penalty falls entirely on women. The parallel verse (4:16) on men prescribes unspecified "punishment" and adds that if men repent, "leave them alone." Women get life imprisonment; men get a conditional warning. The abrogation itself compounds the issue — the original rule was either a genuine divine command later overturned (divine trial-and-error) or was never eternal (undermining the Quran's self-description as eternal law). Either way, the original gender asymmetry remains in the text, never retracted.

Why it fails

Progressive revelation concedes the point: the original rule was neither optimal nor eternal, which contradicts the Quran's self-description as the unchanging word of an omniscient God. The abrogated verse remains in the text, offering no internal signal that it has been overridden. The harsher penalty directed only at women is the fingerprint of 7th-century Arabian patriarchy embedded in divine law.

Historical claim contradicted by 1400 years of history Contradiction Moderate Quran 4:141
"Never will Allah give the disbelievers over the believers a way [to overcome them]."

What the verse says

Allah will never allow disbelievers to gain dominance over believers. This is a falsifiable historical prediction.

Why this is a problem

The prediction has been repeatedly falsified. The Mongols destroyed Baghdad in 1258 and killed the Caliph while themselves non-Muslim. European colonial powers placed most of the Muslim world under non-Muslim rule from the 18th to 20th centuries. By virtually any measure — economic, military, scientific, political — Muslim-majority nations today lag behind non-Muslim ones. If 4:141 is a divine promise, it has been broken across centuries of Muslim history.

Why it fails

The verse says no such thing. It is simple and unconditional: Allah will never give disbelievers a way over believers. Adding qualifying conditions after the fact to rescue the verse from obvious historical falsification is textbook special pleading.

"You did not kill them, but Allah killed them" — moral accountability dissolved Logical Inconsistency Contradiction Moderate Quran 8:17
"And you did not kill them, but it was Allah who killed them. And you threw not when you threw, but it was Allah who threw..."

What the verse says

At the Battle of Badr, when Muslims killed enemies it was actually Allah killing them, and when Muhammad threw dust it was actually Allah throwing it. Human actors are credited with their deeds only nominally.

Why this is a problem

The Quran elsewhere holds people fully responsible for their actions (2:286, 17:15). But 8:17 dissolves Muslim moral agency in battle: killing in Allah's cause is Allah's action, not the human's. This is the theological seed of the "holy warrior" mindset — the fighter does not bear moral responsibility for killings because Allah is the true agent. If Allah does the actions of believers in battle, does He also do the actions of disbelievers who kill believers? If yes, He is killing on both sides. If no, moral agency is preserved for disbelievers but dissolved for believers — an incoherent double standard.

Why it fails

The kasb distinction is a theological scaffold invented centuries after the Quran specifically to manage this problem, and its obscurity is proverbial even within Islamic theology. More critically, jihadist ideology relies on exactly 8:17's logic: the fighter does not bear moral responsibility because Allah is the true agent. If the apologetic reading were obvious, that weaponization would be impossible. The verse plainly says the killing was done by Allah, not by humans.

Military prediction: twenty Muslims defeat two hundred Contradiction Treatment of Disbelievers Moderate Quran 8:65–66
"If there are among you twenty [who are] steadfast, they will overcome two hundred... Now, Allah has lightened [the hardship] for you, and He knows that among you is weakness. So if there are from you one hundred, they will overcome two hundred..."

What the verses say

Steadfast Muslims will defeat ten times their number. In the very next verse, this ratio is revised to two-to-one because Allah "knows there is weakness" in the community.

Why this is a problem

An all-knowing Allah would have known the weakness from the start and set the final ratio at the outset. The revision presents as new knowledge something an omniscient God would have possessed eternally. Furthermore, the 1:2 ratio is an empirically falsifiable military prediction that Islamic history does not consistently support — Muslim armies have frequently been defeated by smaller non-Muslim forces (Mongol invasion, Crusades, European colonial encounters).

Why it fails

The verb khaffafa ("He lightened") is explicitly a revision — the word means a reduction, not a timeless precaution. Either Allah set the original bar knowing it would need reducing (making the initial prescription performatively burdensome) or He learned of the weakness after the fact (compromising omniscience). The verse says what it says, and what it says tracks neither Islamic history nor logical consistency.

Polytheists are "unclean" and forbidden from the sacred mosque Treatment of Disbelievers Moderate Quran 9:28
"O you who have believed, indeed the polytheists are unclean (najas), so let them not approach al-Masjid al-Haram after this, their [final] year."

What the verse says

Polytheists are declared ritually impure — najas, the same legal category as feces, urine, and pigs in Islamic purity law — and permanently banned from the Sacred Mosque in Mecca.

Why this is a problem

This is not metaphor. Saudi Arabia enforces the Mecca ban at city borders to this day, citing 9:28. A Christian or Hindu expatriate worker in Saudi Arabia cannot enter Mecca — ever — because the Quran classifies their religious identity as ritually polluting. Classifying entire categories of human beings as impure by virtue of their religion is structurally identical to caste-based untouchability, which Islam elsewhere condemns.

Why it fails

The "spiritual not physical" reading is a contemporary apologetic frame. Shia Twelver jurisprudence and several Sunni opinions have historically enforced najas as ritual-physical impurity. Saudi Arabia's ongoing physical exclusion of non-Muslims from Mecca applies the verse directly as a matter of state law. Classifying people as polluting by religious identity alone — regardless of personal conduct — is what the verse prescribes, and it remains in force.
